Audience Guide

Juliet
and Romeo

Royal Swedish Ballet — 7 May 2026 — Teatro Real, Madrid

Choreography by Mats Ek

The Choreographer

Mats Ek

Mats Ek (born 1945) is one of the most distinctive and radical voices in contemporary dance. He grew up immersed in the arts — his mother was legendary Swedish choreographer Birgit Cullberg, founder of the Cullberg Ballet; his father the celebrated actor Anders Ek. He trained as both actor and dancer, eventually leading the Cullberg Ballet through the 1980s and 90s.

His work is instantly recognisable: grounded, earthy movement with bent knees and flexed feet, dark humour, and fierce psychological intensity. He has spent his career dismantling the great classical ballets — Giselle, Swan Lake, Sleeping Beauty — stripping away romanticism to expose rawer truths about desire, power, and what it means to be human.

Artistic Influences

  • Birgit Cullberg (his mother)Psychologically driven narrative dance rooted in expressionism — the idea that ballet could tell dark, true human stories.
  • German Expressionism & Pina BauschRaw emotion, grotesque physicality, the subversion of classical forms. Dance-theatre as a vehicle for the unbeautiful.
  • Theatre & acting trainingHis dramatic background gives his work an intensity distinct from pure dance abstraction. His dancers are always fully embodied characters.
  • Feminist & psychoanalytic thoughtHis reworkings of classics consistently expose the power dynamics hidden inside their romantic surfaces.

Photos: Sören Vilks / The Royal Swedish Opera

About the Work

What to expect tonight

The title is reversed deliberately. Juliet comes first. She is the protagonist — more active, more present, more fully drawn than Shakespeare's original.

Ek created this work in 2013 for the Royal Swedish Ballet's 240th anniversary. He rejected Prokofiev's famous score (too loaded with familiar associations) and instead built a new soundscape from fragments of Tchaikovsky — the 5th Symphony, the Manfred Symphony, Piano Concerto No. 1, and more — woven into a continuous emotional arc.

"There is a beauty that is not pretty. The death is definitive, without any element that embellishes it — but even so, the form must evoke the memory of the life that has disappeared."

There is no balcony. No poison. No sword fight. No faked death. Ek strips everything back to the essential: two young people, recognisably modern, trying to find their way out of a hostile world — and what it costs them.

Look for

Movement that feels raw and almost awkward — this is intentional. Ek avoids beautiful-for-its-own-sake. His dancers lurch, collapse, reach, and cling.

Juliet's agency — she drives the story. Watch how her physical vocabulary differs from Romeo's; she is rarely passive.

The ensemble as threat — the society surrounding the lovers is expressed through group scenes that feel oppressive, not decorative.

Costume as character — colour and fabric signal world and status. The swirling, billowing skirts of the ensemble are a deliberate visual spectacle.

An ending that refuses comfort — Ek gives death no poetry. It is final, unadorned, and more devastating for it.

🎵 The music is Tchaikovsky — but not the Prokofiev score you might expect. Listen for familiar melodies arriving in unexpected emotional contexts. The score was arranged by Anders Högstedt and is performed live by the Teatro Real Orchestra.

Photo: Sören Vilks / The Royal Swedish Opera
